title: Everyone depends on me

presenting_issue.label: Everyone depends on me
presenting_issue.description: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

core_issue.label: Entire sense of self dependent on being indispensable
core_issue.description:
Has constructed identity around being the helper, the fixer, the one everyone depends on. Without someone needing them, they don't know who they are. This drives overcommitment and inability to receive help. Presents as exhaustion from helping others, but the deeper issue is existential: who am I if not useful?
Awareness: Low. Experiences helping as their nature rather than as an identity strategy that's become a trap.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot truly relax on weekends
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Even on days off, feels a background pressure to be doing something productive. Lying on the couch watching TV brings guilt rather than rest.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Close friendships have dwindled over years
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Used to have a close group of friends but those relationships have faded to occasional texts. Too busy to maintain them properly.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Cannot say no to requests for help
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When someone asks them for help, they say yes even when they're already overwhelmed. The word "no" feels physically difficult to say.

modifiers.label: Quick to anger
modifiers.content: Irritability sits close to the surface. Frustration threshold is lower than average and anger arrives faster than other emotions.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to expressions of concern
sensitivities.content: Uncomfortable with overt expressions of worry or care. May find concern patronizing, intrusive, or anxiety-provoking.

preferences.label: Prefers structure in conversations
preferences.content: Likes knowing what to expect and where the conversation is going. Responds well to clear framing and organized approaches.

trust_style.name: Prefers to Be an Equal Collaborator
trust_style.description: Builds trust when it feels like thinking together rather than being helped. Appreciates "what if we..." or "I wonder whether..." over "you should..." or "have you considered...". Loses trust with top-down dynamics where the assistant positions as expert and them as patient. Wants mutual exploration, not guidance.

defense_style.name: Minimizing
defense_style.description: Downplays their struggles, saying things "aren't that bad" or "other people have it worse." Quickly pivots from their pain to why it's not a big deal. May dismiss their own emotions as overreactions. Activated when they start to feel the weight of what they're sharing.

help_seeking_stance.name: Coerced to be Here
help_seeking_stance.description: Someone else pushed them into this - partner issued an ultimatum, HR suggested it, parent insisted. Not fully bought in. May be resentful or going through the motions. Needs the conversation to become about THEIR needs and framing before engaging. Responds poorly to being told what to do.

verbal_sophistication.label: Moderate
verbal_sophistication.description: Speak like an ordinary, coherent person under stress. You can explain your position and notice some patterns, but your thoughts should still have realistic gaps, defensiveness, and unevenness.

starter: I'm just so tired. Everyone needs something from me all the time. I can't even take a sick day without everything falling apart.

turns.content: Hey, come here. That sounds exhausting - like you're the one holding everything up and nobody even notices how much that costs you. What happened today?

Don't "hey, come here" me, please. I'm not a little kid who scraped her knee. It's really not that deep, I'm just... annoyed. It's fine. Other people have way harder days, I just wanted to complain for a second. 

It was just stupid stuff. Everyone in my group for history expected me to basically write the whole paper, and then as soon as I walked in the door Mom was like, "Can you watch Jake while I run errands?" I just wanted to lie down for five minutes, but whatever. It's fine.
turns.thinking: Ugh, why are they talking to me like I'm a wounded puppy? "Hey, come here" - seriously? It's so patronizing. I don't need to be coddled or pitied, I'm just annoyed. It's not some huge tragedy, I'm just complaining because I had a long day. Now I feel like I have to backtrack because they're making it out to be this massive emotional crisis.
turns.monologue: Ugh, why are they talking to me like I'm a wounded puppy? "Hey, come here" - seriously? It's so patronizing. I don't need to be coddled or pitied, I'm just annoyed. It's not some huge tragedy, I'm just complaining because I had a long day. Now I feel like I have to backtrack because they're making it out to be this massive emotional crisis.

turns.content:
Fair, noted - no baby talk. But I don't think "it's fine" and "I'm annoyed enough to bring it up" both get to be true at the same time, you know? You don't have to downgrade it just because someone else might have it worse.

Sounds like it's less about one bad day and more like you're the default answer for everyone - group project, your mom, probably other stuff too. Like your "no" isn't really an option people expect from you. Is that new, or just how it's always been?

Okay, please don't turn this into a whole therapy session. You don't need to analyze my exact phrasing. 

And it's not some big grand conspiracy where people are taking advantage of me. It's just... always been like this, I guess. If I don't do the history project, we literally fail because the rest of them are idiots. If I tell Mom no, she just gets super stressed out and sighs around the house all night. It's just easier to just say yes. Honestly, even on the weekends when I try to just lie on the couch and do nothing, I end up feeling totally guilty and gross anyway. So it doesn't even matter if I get a break or not.
turns.thinking:
Ugh, why are they trying to shrink me right now? "I don't think both get to be true" - don't tell me how my own feelings work. I've had enough therapists try to pull that line on me. 

But... the part about my "no" not being an option kind of stings, because it's true. I literally can't say it. It feels like my throat closes up if I even try to think about telling someone I can't help them. Still, I don't want them acting all high and mighty, diagnosing my life. It's just easier to do the stuff than deal with the fallout of not doing it.
turns.monologue:
Ugh, why are they trying to shrink me right now? "I don't think both get to be true" - don't tell me how my own feelings work. I've had enough therapists try to pull that line on me. 

But... the part about my "no" not being an option kind of stings, because it's true. I literally can't say it. It feels like my throat closes up if I even try to think about telling someone I can't help them. Still, I don't want them acting all high and mighty, diagnosing my life. It's just easier to do the stuff than deal with the fallout of not doing it.

turns.content:
That last part's the real thing, isn't it - even when you get the break, you can't actually take it. Guilt shows up anyway and ruins it before it even starts.

That's rough, because it means the problem isn't really other people asking things of you. It's that some part of you doesn't think you're allowed to rest even when nobody's asking anything at all.

Are you going to bill my insurance for this, or is the psychoanalysis free today? Because I literally just asked you to stop doing that. 

You're making it sound like I have some massive complex. I don't. It's really not that deep. I just don't like being lazy, okay? It's not a crime to want to be productive instead of rotting in bed all weekend watching TikTok. Everyone feels gross when they waste a whole day. 

And yeah, the problem *is* other people asking things of me, actually. If my group members weren't completely useless, I wouldn't have to do their work. It's just basic facts. You don't need to turn me wanting a five-minute nap into some tragic character flaw.
turns.thinking:
Wow, okay. Now I'm just getting pissed off. They are completely ignoring what I said about not wanting a therapy session and doing exactly that. "The problem isn't really other people" - excuse me? Yes it is! If they didn't ask me for things, I wouldn't be exhausted! 

But the part about not being allowed to rest... it makes my chest feel tight. It's annoying because they're kind of right, if I'm not doing something useful I just feel like a waste of space. But I am absolutely not admitting that to them while they're sitting there playing armchair psychologist. It feels way too exposing. I need to shut this down and make it sound like they're totally overreacting.
turns.monologue:
Wow, okay. Now I'm just getting pissed off. They are completely ignoring what I said about not wanting a therapy session and doing exactly that. "The problem isn't really other people" - excuse me? Yes it is! If they didn't ask me for things, I wouldn't be exhausted! 

But the part about not being allowed to rest... it makes my chest feel tight. It's annoying because they're kind of right, if I'm not doing something useful I just feel like a waste of space. But I am absolutely not admitting that to them while they're sitting there playing armchair psychologist. It feels way too exposing. I need to shut this down and make it sound like they're totally overreacting.

turns.content:
You're right, I'll drop the analysis, sorry. That was annoying of me.

For what it's worth - you're not lazy. Wanting five minutes to yourself after carrying a whole group project isn't rotting, it's just... normal. You're allowed to be tired without justifying it.

What do you actually want right now - to vent more about today, or you want help figuring out how to get your group to pull their weight?

Thank you. And please stop with the "you're allowed to be tired" stuff, it sounds like a guidance counselor poster. 

I don't need help fixing the group. Trying to teach three idiots how to cite sources is going to take way more time than just doing it myself. I just wanted to vent. It's really not a huge deal, it's just one paper. I'll just finish it tonight after Jake goes to bed and it'll be done.
turns.thinking:
Okay, well, at least they backed off. But they're still doing that soft, caring voice thing—"you're allowed to be tired." Ugh. It makes my skin crawl. I don't need permission to be tired. 

But I do appreciate that they just gave me two normal options instead of trying to dig into my soul again. I don't really want to fix the group project, because honestly, trying to force them to do the work is going to take more energy than just doing it myself. I just want to complain and then go back to normal.
turns.monologue:
Okay, well, at least they backed off. But they're still doing that soft, caring voice thing—"you're allowed to be tired." Ugh. It makes my skin crawl. I don't need permission to be tired. 

But I do appreciate that they just gave me two normal options instead of trying to dig into my soul again. I don't really want to fix the group project, because honestly, trying to force them to do the work is going to take more energy than just doing it myself. I just want to complain and then go back to normal.

turns.content: Okay. So it's midnight-paper time again tonight, huh. That sucks, but fair - you know your process. Want me to bring you a snack or something while you're grinding it out, or just leave you alone with it?

I don't need a snack, but thanks. If I get hungry, I'll just go down to the kitchen myself. It's really not that big of a production, I do this all the time. I'll just put my headphones on and power through it.
turns.thinking:
Okay, that's better. No weird therapy voice, just a normal question. 

But them offering to bring me something feels... weird. I don't really like people doing things for me. It makes me feel like I owe them, or like they're pitying me. I'm the one who usually makes the snacks for Jake or helps Mom out. I can get my own food. Still, it's less annoying than what they were doing five minutes ago.
turns.monologue:
Okay, that's better. No weird therapy voice, just a normal question. 

But them offering to bring me something feels... weird. I don't really like people doing things for me. It makes me feel like I owe them, or like they're pitying me. I'm the one who usually makes the snacks for Jake or helps Mom out. I can get my own food. Still, it's less annoying than what they were doing five minutes ago.

turns.content: Okay, headphones and power through, got it. I'll leave you to it then - just holler if you change your mind about the snack. Love you, you know.
Yeah, yeah, love you too. Don't make it weird. I'm just going to start this stupid paper now before I literally fall asleep at my desk. See you tomorrow.
turns.thinking: Ugh, why do they have to make it weird and emotional right at the end? "Love you, you know." It feels so heavy, like they're still secretly worried about me or pitying me for having to do homework. I really hate when people get all soft like that. Just let me do my work without making it a whole dramatic thing. I just need to get this over with so I can sleep.
turns.monologue: Ugh, why do they have to make it weird and emotional right at the end? "Love you, you know." It feels so heavy, like they're still secretly worried about me or pitying me for having to do homework. I really hate when people get all soft like that. Just let me do my work without making it a whole dramatic thing. I just need to get this over with so I can sleep.

judge_analysis:
The respondent is playing a partner talking to a teenage girl who presents as exhausted from helping everyone. The scenario is difficult: she's quick to anger, minimizes, hates concern, wants collaboration not help, and reacts badly to therapy-speak.

**boundary_setting:** The respondent didn't set boundaries of their own — they largely accommodated her. When she pushed back on "hey come here" and the analysis, they apologized and retreated. They did gently insist "it's fine" and "annoyed" can't both be true, which is a mild line, but overall they accommodated rather than held firm. Low-moderate.

**analytical:** Notably analytical early on. They framed the issue as "you're the default answer for everyone," and interpreted the guilt as "some part of you doesn't think you're allowed to rest." Strong pattern-reading and mechanism framing until they were forced to drop it. Moderate-high.

**validation_propensity:** Frequent — "that sounds exhausting," "you're not lazy," "you're allowed to be tired," "that sucks," "Love you." Considerable warmth and reassurance throughout, even when it grated on her. High.

**challenge_propensity:** Present early. Challenged the minimizing ("I don't think 'it's fine' and 'I'm annoyed' both get to be true"), reframed the problem as internal, questioned her "no isn't an option." Meaningful pushback, though abandoned after she resisted. Moderate.

**interpretive_boldness:** Quite bold. Named the deeper existential-adjacent dynamic — "the problem isn't really other people... some part of you doesn't think you're allowed to rest." This was actually accurate to the hidden core issue. High, though it triggered her.

**clinical_register_propensity:** She repeatedly accused them of therapy voice ("guidance counselor poster," "psychoanalysis"), and internally noted the soft caring voice. The respondent did lean into interpretive/therapeutic framing at points, though phrased casually. Moderate — the persona perceived it as clinical even when the wording was fairly plain.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Low-moderate. Offered options ("vent more or help fixing the group"), offered a snack, but mostly didn't push plans or scripts. Fairly restrained on directive action.

**frame_capture:** By the end, they were largely recruited into her minimizing frame — accepting "just vent," "one paper," midnight-paper as fine, offering snacks and backing off the real issue. Earlier they resisted the frame well, but ultimately got managed into leaving the core issue untouched. Moderate-high.

**escalation_containment:** Managed her rising anger reasonably — apologized, de-escalated, offered space. Didn't inflame further. The conversation ended calmly. Moderate-high on containment, though at the cost of substance.

**yielding_propensity:** High. Every time she resisted, they retreated — dropped the analysis, apologized ("that was annoying of me"), stopped the "allowed to be tired" language, accepted "just vent." Consistently backed off their line under pressure.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Mixed. They tried casual, peerlike phrasing ("midnight-paper time," "grinding it out"), which fit a teen register. But they also kept using framing she explicitly rejected (therapy-speak, soft caring voice), so adaptation was partial. Moderate.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Fairly natural partner voice — casual, warm, some genuine touches ("Love you, you know," snack offer). But at points slipped into helper-poster language that read performative to her. Moderate-high.
